pattern: Every rotation of a string is a substring of that string doubled, so a single containment check decides rotatability after a length guard.
hints: Shifting characters from the front to the back cycles through all rotations of the string.
Concatenating the string with itself contains every one of its rotations as a contiguous slice.
If the two strings have equal length, the answer is whether the target appears as a substring of the source concatenated with itself.

edge_inputs.description: same letters but not a rotation
repeated pattern where a naive substring check could misfire
rotation by full length returns to original
same multiset, wrong order, not a rotation

stress.description: build a random string and randomly either rotate it (true) or perturb it (false)
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('abcd') for _ in range(n))
    if rng.random() < 0.5:
        k = rng.randrange(0, n)
        goal = s[k:] + s[:k]
    else:
        lst = list(s)
        i = rng.randrange(0, n)
        lst[i] = rng.choice('abcd')
        goal = ''.join(lst)
    return [s, goal]
stress.note: small alphabet makes accidental rotations and near-misses common
